claims.claim_text: The opening fintech outage anecdote presents a specific production mistake as a real event without attribution.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 1
claims.anchor_text: A mid-sized financial technology company relied on a shared network drive to store their infrastructure configuration files. Engineers would map the drive, open the files in their local editors, make changes, and save them back to the network. One Friday afternoon, a junior engineer was tasked with updating a testing environment. By mistake, they opened the production `payment-processor-deployment.yaml` file instead of the testing variant. They modified the container image tag to an experimental build, saved the file, and applied the configuration to the cluster.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Teams that manage infrastructure files on a shared drive without version control can easily change the wrong environment by mistake and have no reliable audit trail when something breaks.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed incident narrative with named artifacts and operational specifics, so the false precision should be removed.

claims.claim_text: The module claims that the outage halted all transactions for six hours and caused millions in losses.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 2
claims.anchor_text: Within minutes, the payment processing system began dropping transactions. When the senior engineers rushed to investigate, they looked at the configuration file on the network drive, but it only showed the current, broken state. Because there was no version control system in place, there was no historical record of what the file looked like ten minutes prior, who had changed it, or why. The team had to manually reconstruct the production configuration from memory and outdated documentation. The subsequent delay in restoring service halted all customer transactions for six hours, resulting in millions of dollars in lost revenue and severe reputational damage.
claims.suggested_rewrite: When the system failed, the team could see only the current broken file state. Without version control, they had to reconstruct the previous configuration manually, which slowed recovery and made the incident more damaging.
claims.rationale: These are concrete loss and duration claims about an unattributed incident, so they must be rewritten without invented numbers.

claims.claim_text: Git is the industry-standard version control system.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 3
claims.rationale: This is a comparative popularity claim and should point to a source rather than stand unsupported.

claims.claim_text: The module says modern CI/CD pipelines cannot exist without Git.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 4
claims.anchor_text: Without Git, modern continuous integration and continuous deployment (CI/CD) pipelines cannot exist.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Modern CI/CD pipelines rely on a version-controlled source of truth, and Git is the system most teams use for that job.
claims.rationale: The current sentence is an unnecessary absolute claim because CI/CD is possible with other version-control systems.

claims.claim_text: The module says Git refuses to create commits unless user.name and user.email are set.
claims.anchor_text: Because Git is designed for collaboration across potentially thousands of developers, it refuses to create snapshots unless it knows exactly who is taking them. Every commit requires an author name and an email address.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Git records author and committer identity in each commit, and in normal day-to-day use you should configure `user.name` and `user.email` so your commits are attributed correctly.
claims.rationale: Git can derive identity from other sources in some setups, so the current wording is too absolute.

claims.claim_text: The AWS secret leak story claims bots found the key within 120 seconds and caused a $65,000 bill overnight.
claims.span_hint: section: The Snapshot Lifecycle, War Story
claims.anchor_text: They then pushed the code to a public GitHub repository. Within 120 seconds, automated security bots scanning public GitHub repositories found the exposed AWS key. The bots instantly spun up hundreds of massive cryptocurrency mining servers across multiple AWS regions using the compromised credentials. By the time the developer woke up the next morning, the company had incurred a $65,000 cloud billing charge.
claims.suggested_rewrite: They then pushed the code to a public repository. Exposed cloud credentials in public repositories can be discovered quickly by automated scanners and abused to create expensive resources before a team notices.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed security incident with fake-precise timing and cost data, so it must be generalized.

claims.claim_text: Git object names are traditionally SHA-1 values, and newer repositories can use SHA-256.
claims.rationale: The upstream Git documentation directly covers the SHA-1-to-SHA-256 transition, but the primary host is currently off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: git pull runs git fetch and then integrates the result into the current branch.
claims.rationale: The authoritative explanation is in the Git manpage on git-scm.com, which is off the allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The module says Linus Torvalds wrote the initial version of Git in 14 days and the Linux kernel moved to it two months later.
claims.anchor_text: In 2005, the Linux kernel community abruptly lost their free license to a proprietary version control system. Linus Torvalds, the original creator of Linux, needed a replacement immediately. Unimpressed with existing options, he wrote the initial version of Git in just 14 days, and the massive Linux kernel codebase migrated to it two months later.
claims.suggested_rewrite: In 2005, the Linux kernel project lost its free BitKeeper arrangement, which pushed Linus Torvalds and the community to create Git quickly as a replacement.
claims.rationale: The exact 14-day and two-month timelines are more precise than the available beginner-safe sourcing supports here.

claims.claim_text: The module says Git's name is British slang and attributes a self-deprecating quote to Torvalds.
claims.rationale: Historical etymology and attributed quotations should be sourced.

claims.claim_text: The module says Git does not track empty directories and that developers conventionally use .gitkeep to force tracking.
claims.anchor_text: Due to its underlying database architecture, which maps paths directly to file contents, Git only tracks files. If you create an empty directory and run `git status`, Git will completely ignore it. Developers work around this limitation by placing a hidden, empty file (often conventionally named `.gitkeep`) inside a directory to force Git to track the folder's existence.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Git does not track empty directories on their own. If a team wants an otherwise empty directory to exist in the repository, it usually adds a small placeholder file inside it.
claims.rationale: The empty-directory limitation is real, but `.gitkeep` is a convention rather than an official Git feature and should be phrased that way.

claims.claim_text: The module says accidental Git hash collisions are astronomically unlikely and uses a lightning-and-lottery analogy.
claims.anchor_text: The hashes identifying your commits (the SHA-1 strings) are 40-character hexadecimal numbers. The mathematical probability of two different snapshots generating the exact same hash (a hash collision) is astronomically low. You are statistically far more likely to be struck by lightning while simultaneously winning the lottery than to experience an accidental Git hash collision in your repository.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Historically, Git has identified objects with 40-character SHA-1 values. Accidental collisions are extremely unlikely in normal use, but SHA-1 is no longer considered strong enough for long-term cryptographic collision resistance, which is one reason newer Git work supports SHA-256.
claims.rationale: The current wording overstates SHA-1 safety and uses an uncited analogy that obscures modern collision concerns.

further_reading.title: Git Guide
further_reading.why_relevant: A concise beginner-friendly overview of Git concepts, commands, and workflow.

further_reading.title: Git Guides: git pull
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains remote synchronization in the same beginner-friendly register as this module.

further_reading.title: github/gitignore
further_reading.why_relevant: Provides practical `.gitignore` examples learners can reuse immediately.

notes: Quiz items and hands-on exercise steps were intentionally excluded. Several exact Git behavior claims have primary documentation on git-scm.com; those were marked `needs_allowlist_expansion` when the claim itself was sound.
